Lady Xieof[Sun] Quan, the Lord ofWu, was fromShanyin[山陰],Kuaiji[會稽].
Her father,[Xie] Jiong, wasGentleman of the SecretariatandPrefectofXu[徐令] underHan.A
The son of[Xie] Jiong,[Xie] Cheng, authored theBook of Later Han. He appraised[Xie] Jiongof being humane and filial in conduct from a young age and that he was wise and understanding, possessing outstanding talent. The younger brother of[Xie] Jiongwas[Xie] Zhen, who followed rules and regulations. He focused on learning and valued justice. He was nominated asFilial and IncorruptandChiefofJianchang[建昌長], dying in office.
The mother of[Sun] Quan,[Lady] Wu, engaged her to[Sun] Quanas aConsort. She had love and attention, being favored. When[Sun] Quanlater marriedLady Xu, who was the granddaughter of his aunt, he wanted to have[Lady] Xiebe inferior to her.[Lady] Xiewas unwilling to be so. Hence, there was a loss of will.1She diedearly.•••
It is unknown if the “will” [志] was that ofSun QuanorLady Xie. The former interpretation goes well with the prior sentence "[Lady] Xie was unwilling to be so” asSun Quanwould be unhappy with her, while the latter goes well with the following sentence “she died early” asLady Xiewould be unhappy and frustrated, and perhaps die of depression. As such, I did not assign a pronoun before “will” to account for this vagueness.↩
***
Over ten years later, her younger brother,[Xie] Cheng, was conferred to serve asPalace Gentleman for All Purposes, then promoted toChief Commandantof the East Sector ofChangsha[長沙東部都尉], thenGrand AdministratorofWuling[武陵太守]. He authored over one hundred volumes of theBook of Later Han.B
TheOrganized Records of Kuaijistates:[Xie] Chengwas styledWeiping. He had rich learning and extensive knowledge such that once he knew something through his sight, he would never forget it until his end. His son,[Xie] Chong, served asGeneral who Displays Might, and the younger brother of[Xie] Chong,[Xie] Xu, served asGrand AdministratorofWu commandery[吳郡太守], both men being famous.

Updates of this biography have been discontinued by the translator in favor of an alternate translation found inRobert Joe Cutter and William G. Crowell’sEmpresses and Consorts, a marvelous hardcover book detailing the role of women during the Three Kingdoms era. It includes the biographies of women included in Chen Shou’sSanguozhi.
This translation remains online for archival purposes only.
Lady Xie, wife of Quan, Lord of Wu, was a native of Shanyin in Kuaiji. Her father, Ju, was Prefect of the Masters of Writing and Prefect of Xu.AThe mother of Quan, Lady Wu, took Lady Xie to be Quan’s consort, and loved her greatly. Later, when Quan took Lady Xu, the granddaughter of his aunt, to be wife, he wanted to make Lady Xie treat her as her superior. Xie refused and became depressed. She died early. More than ten years later, her younger brother, Cheng, was made Gentleman of the Officers, and later was transferred to be Eastern Colonel of Changsha and Grand Administrator of Wuling. He compiled more than a hundred chapters of the Hou Hanshu.B
Ju’s son, Cheng, in theHou Han shuthat he compiled, praised Ju to be benevolent and filial in his youth, and being of great understanding and intelligence he had the talents of a Prefect. Zhen, the younger brother of Ju, was heedful of the law, making learning and righteousness his priorities. He was recommended as a “Filially Pious and Incorrupt”, and made chief of Jianchang. He died in office.
FromKuaiji Dianlu: Cheng, styled Weiping, was of great learning and broad knowledge. Once he saw something he would remember it for life. His son Song was General Who Manifests Power, and Song’s younger brother Mao was Grand Administrator of Wu commandery. Both were men of renown.
